era_context: The year 938 CE falls within the early 10th century, a period of significant political fragmentation and realignment across Eurasia. In East Asia, the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period was at its height, with Vietnam gaining independence from Chinese rule. The Byzantine Empire under Constantine VII was consolidating power. The Islamic world was fragmented among competing caliphates and dynasties. In Europe, feudal kingdoms were consolidating power from the remnants of the Carolingian Empire.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern and Eastern Europe beyond the Rus' and German spheres (sparse documentation)
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited primary sources)
Mesoamerica and South America (no written records for this specific year)

events.title: Battle of Bach Dang River; Vietnamese independence from Chinese rule
events.region: Vietnam (Dai Co Viet), Red River Delta
events.description: Ngo Quyen, a Vietnamese general, defeated the Southern Han Dynasty at the Battle of Bach Dang River in 938 CE. This victory marked the end of Chinese rule over Vietnam and the establishment of Vietnamese independence. According to Vietnamese tradition, Ngo Quyen used a clever military stratagem, placing iron-tipped stakes in the river to trap the Southern Han fleet. The victory became a legendary moment in Vietnamese national consciousness and is celebrated as the founding moment of independent Vietnam. Ngo Quyen subsequently established the Ngo Dynasty and consolidated his rule over the Red River Delta.
key_figures: Ngo Quyen (Vietnamese general and founder of the Ngo Dynasty)

sources.name: Dai Viet Su Ky Toan Thu (Complete History of Great Viet), compiled 13th century

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Bach Dang River is attested in both Vietnamese and Chinese sources. The date 938 CE is traditional and widely accepted, though some sources place it in 939 CE. The battle's legendary details (iron stakes, etc.) are likely embellished, but the core fact of the victory is secure.
cross_references: Marks the beginning of Vietnamese independence (938–1427 CE)
Part of the broader fragmentation of Chinese power during the Five Dynasties period
Leads to the establishment of the Ngo Dynasty (938–968 CE)

events.title: Shi Jingtang consolidates Later Jin Dynasty in northern China
events.description: Shi Jingtang, a Shatuo Turk general, had established the Later Jin Dynasty in 936 CE with Khitan Liao support. By 938 CE, he was consolidating his control over northern China, particularly the Yellow River valley. Later Jin was one of the Five Dynasties that ruled northern China during the fragmentation following the Later Tang collapse. The dynasty was characterized by its reliance on Khitan military support and its competition with other regional powers (Southern Tang, Northern Han, etc.). Shi Jingtang's reign would be brief (936–942 CE), but Later Jin represented an important phase in the eventual reunification of China.
key_figures: Shi Jingtang (Emperor Gaozu of Later Jin, r. 936–942)
Elhot (Khitan Liao ruler, Later Jin's ally)

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government) by Sima Guang
Official histories of the Five Dynasties period

events.certainty_note: Shi Jingtang's consolidation of Later Jin is well-documented in Chinese historical records. The dating is secure.
cross_references: Part of the Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period (907–979 CE)
Precedes the Song Dynasty's reunification of China (960 CE)

events.title: Byzantine-Rus' diplomatic relations; Igor of Kiev's embassy to Constantinople
events.description: Following the failed Varangian raid on Constantinople in 941 CE (or possibly in 938 CE, depending on the source), diplomatic relations between Kievan Rus' and the Byzantine Empire were being negotiated. Igor of Kiev sent embassies to Constantinople to establish trade and military agreements. These diplomatic contacts would eventually lead to the Christianization of Rus' in the late 10th century. The relationship between Rus' and Byzantium was characterized by a mixture of military conflict and commercial cooperation, with the Varangians serving as both raiders and mercenaries.
key_figures: Igor of Kiev (Prince of Kievan Rus')

sources.name: De Administrando Imperio by Constantine VII Porphyrogennetos
Primary Chronicle (Povest' vremennykh let), compiled 12th century

events.certainty_note: The dating of Igor's embassy and the Varangian raid is uncertain. Some sources place the raid in 938 CE, others in 941 CE. The diplomatic contacts are attested in Byzantine sources, but the specific year is approximate.
cross_references: Precedes Igor's Treaty with Constantinople (945 CE)
Part of the broader development of Rus'-Byzantine relations

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Bach Dang River is presented in Vietnamese historiography as a decisive, sudden victory; in reality, Vietnamese independence was a gradual process, and Chinese cultural and political influence remained strong in Vietnam for centuries. The legendary details of the battle (iron stakes, etc.) are likely embellished or invented by later historians. The dating of the battle is uncertain; some sources place it in 939 CE rather than 938 CE. The Varangian raid on Constantinople is sometimes dated to 938 CE rather than 941 CE, and the significance of the raid is often overstated; Byzantine sources indicate it was repelled relatively quickly.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 938 CE is moderate but regionally concentrated. Chinese sources are abundant for the Five Dynasties period, though they were compiled during the Song Dynasty and may reflect Song-era biases. Vietnamese sources for the Battle of Bach Dang River are legendary and were written centuries after the events; the earliest detailed accounts date to the 13th century. Byzantine sources provide information about Rus'-Byzantine relations, but the specific dating of events is uncertain. Sub-Saharan Africa, Northern Europe, and the Americas are absent from the historical record for this year.

graph_edges.note: Ngo Quyen's victory at Bach Dang River in 938 CE established the Ngo Dynasty, which collapsed in 968 CE
Later Jin's consolidation in 938 CE was part of the process that eventually led to the Song Dynasty's reunification of China in 960 CE
Diplomatic contacts in 938 CE led to Igor's Treaty with Constantinople in 945 CE
